title: Keeping rats and pests out of a compost heap
subtitle: What attracts rodents, what deters them, and how to manage an existing infestation
excerpt: Rats visit compost heaps because food is available, not because of the compost itself. Removing the food source is more effective than any deterrent product. A properly managed heap with no cooked food, no exposed layers, and good structure is unattractive to rats. If rats are already present, there are several practical steps to remove them without poison.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on composting and pests (2024). Pest Control Technology UK guidance on compost bin rat management. British Pest Control Association advisory on rodent deterrence. WRAP composting guidance for households (2022).

content.text: Rats need food, water, and shelter. An open compost heap with kitchen scraps near the surface provides all three. The solution is to remove the food access, not to abandon composting. Over 90 percent of rat visits to compost bins stop within two weeks of removing cooked or processed food.
What attracts rats
Cooked food, meat, fish, dairy, and bread on or near the surface.
Citrus peel in large quantities left near the surface (the high sugar content).
A heap that is never turned, providing a stable, undisturbed nesting environment.
An open base in contact with the ground with no mesh lining.
Preventive measures
Do not compost cooked food in an open heap. Use a sealed Bokashi system or green cone digester for cooked food waste.
Bury each kitchen scrap addition under at least 10 cm of brown material (cardboard, woody prunings, dried leaves).
Fit a 6 mm wire mesh base under the bin, folded up 15 cm on each side. This prevents burrowing from below.
Turn the heap every 2 to 4 weeks. Turning disturbs any nesting and makes the heap less attractive as a winter shelter.
Site the bin away from fence lines and walls where rats travel. An open position in the middle of the garden is harder for rats to approach undetected.
If rats are already present
Stop adding kitchen waste immediately. Rats will vacate within 1 to 2 weeks if the food source is removed.
Turn the heap fully, pulling it out of the bin and restacking. This destroys any nest structure.
Fit mesh underneath and around the base of the bin after moving it.
Contact your local council if rats persist. Most councils offer rodent control at low or no cost under the Prevention of Damage by Pests Act 1949.
What does not work
Peppermint oil and ultrasonic deterrents: there is no evidence these work.
Rat poison in or around the bin: this kills secondary predators (foxes, owls, cats) and is banned for amateur use in some formulations. It does not address the root cause.
